In the Beginning: The Norse Creation: Before the world existed, two very different places faced each other across an empty void. What were they, and what made each one so extreme? What happened when they finally met in the middle?

Odin, Vili, and Ve built the entire world out of one giant's body. Pick two of the things they made and explain what part of Ymir they used. Why do you think the Norse imagined the world being built this way instead of simply appearing out of nothing?

At the very end of the story, a squirrel named Ratatoskr runs up and down the World Tree all day delivering insults between an eagle and a serpent. Why do you think the Norse included this detail in their creation story? What does it tell you about how they saw the universe?
The Nine Worlds of Yggdrasil: The nine worlds are all connected by Yggdrasil — the World Tree — and a serpent is constantly gnawing at its roots while everything else depends on it holding together. What do you think the World Tree represents? Why might the Norse have imagined the universe as something fragile that requires constant maintenance?

Each of the nine worlds has a very different character — fire, ice, light, dark, the living, the dead. If you could visit one of the nine worlds for a day (other than Midgard, where you already live), which would you choose and why? What would you want to find there?

The dwarves of Svartalfheim made almost every great weapon and treasure in the myths — Mjolnir, Gleipnir, Odin's spear — and yet they rarely appear as main characters. Why do you think the makers of the most important things often stay in the background of the stories?
Odin and the Price of Wisdom: Odin gave up his eye to drink from Mimir's Well — and then, later, he hung on the World Tree for nine days to find the runes. Why do you think wisdom required such extreme sacrifices? Do you think it was worth it? Why or why not?

When Odin finally drinks from the well, he sees the future — including how he will die. Knowing that terrible things are coming and being unable to stop them, he goes back to Asgard and keeps going anyway. What does that tell you about his character?

Odin's two ravens are named Thought and Memory. Every day they fly across all nine worlds and come back to tell him everything they've seen. Why do you think the story gave those specific names to the ravens? What does it suggest about what Odin values most?
Thor and the Theft of Mjölnir: Thor absolutely did not want to dress as a bride — but he did it anyway. What finally convinced him? What does that decision tell you about what kind of god Thor really is underneath all the thunder and strength?

Loki was the one who came up with the plan, guided the whole mission, and talked their way through the feast with quick thinking. But Thor is the one who got Mjolnir back. Who do you think deserves more credit for the success — Thor or Loki? Use evidence from the story to support your answer.

At the end, Heimdall sees Thor arrive in a tattered wedding dress and says absolutely nothing. Why do you think the story included that detail? What does Heimdall's silence tell us about him — and maybe about friendship?
Freyja and the Necklace of the Brisings: The dwarves set a price for Brisingamen that was designed to be insulting — to test whether Freyja's pride was stronger than her desire. Freyja thought about it and then said yes. Do you think she made the right choice? What does her decision reveal about her character?

Freyja is described as 'the most powerful person in any room she entered' — and also as someone carrying deep grief over her missing husband Od. How do those two things fit together? Can someone be both very powerful and very sad at the same time?

Odin used Brisingamen as leverage to make Freyja do something she didn't choose. She did it, got the necklace back, and walked out without looking back. What do you think Freyja felt in that moment — and why do you think the story ends there, without telling us?
Freyr and the Sword That Fought Alone: Freyr gave away his enchanted sword — his most powerful weapon, the one that would have saved him at Ragnarok — because it was the only thing he had to offer Gerd. Do you think love is a good reason to give up the thing that protects you? What would you have done in his place?

The Norse told this story knowing the whole time that Freyr would die at Ragnarok because he no longer had his sword. Why do you think they told it anyway — and even told it with warmth? What does it say about how they felt about love versus safety?

Skirnir is the one who actually did the hard work — riding through fire, finding Gerd, persuading her. But the story is about Freyr. What role does Skirnir play, and why do you think a good friend who helps you isn't always the center of the story?
Heimdall and the Rainbow Bridge: Heimdall's entire job is to watch — every day, all day, all night, without sleep, without missing anything. He never goes on adventures. He just guards the bridge. Why do you think the Norse made him a god rather than just a very reliable guard? What does giving him divine status say about how they valued this kind of work?

The story mentions that Heimdall wandered through the human world in disguise and became the father of all the different kinds of people. What do you think that story is trying to say — about where people come from and what connects very different kinds of humans to each other?

Gjallarhorn has never been blown. Heimdall is always ready, always waiting, and the horn stays silent. How does knowing that the horn will eventually be blown — at Ragnarok — change the feeling of every quiet day Heimdall spends at his post?
Loki the Trickster: Loki caused most of the problems he ended up solving. Think about the wall-builder story: Loki agreed to the deal, which created the crisis, and then fixed it in the strangest possible way. Do you think the gods were right to keep trusting him after things like this? Why or why not?

Loki had three children who were taken from him and sent away — Fenrir bound underground, Jormungand thrown into the ocean, Hel sent to govern the dead. The story says Loki watched all of this and said nothing. Why do you think that moment matters? How might it connect to what Loki does later?

Loki is described as someone who 'was always both' — both the helper and the danger, both the cause and the solution. Can you think of a real person — from history, a book, or your own life — who reminds you of Loki? What makes someone like that so hard to simply label as good or bad?
Týr and the Binding of Fenrir: Tyr was the only god who fed Fenrir every day — and that daily act of reliability was what made Fenrir trust him enough to agree to the binding test. What does that tell you about how trust is built? And what do you think it felt like for Tyr when that trust ended the way it did?

When no other god would put their hand in Fenrir's mouth, Tyr stepped forward — not impulsively, but calmly, knowing exactly what would happen. What is the difference between bravery and recklessness? Which one was Tyr showing, and how can you tell?

Tyr lost his hand doing something necessary for everyone else's safety — and then went back to his work without complaining. Tuesday is named after him. Why do you think the Norse honored this kind of quiet, costly courage with a day of the week? What does that choice say about their values?
Baldur the Beautiful: Frigg went to every single thing in the nine worlds to make them swear not to hurt Baldur — except the mistletoe, which she thought was too small to bother with. Have you ever overlooked something small that turned out to matter? What does this story suggest about being thorough?

Hel said she would release Baldur if every being in all nine worlds wept for him — and everything did, except one. Why do you think one refusal was enough to keep Baldur in Helheim? Does that seem fair? What do you think the story is trying to say?

The Norse knew from the beginning that Baldur would come back after Ragnarok — that the darkness wasn't permanent. How does knowing that ending change the way you feel about Baldur's death in the middle of the story? Does hope make grief easier to bear?
The Death of Baldur and Loki's Punishmen: Loki ran from Asgard knowing what was coming — he even sat by his fire making a fishing net and thinking through how the gods might catch him. What does that preparation tell you about how smart Loki was? And does being smart make what he did better or worse?

Sigyn, Loki's wife, stayed in the cave beside him and held the bowl. The story doesn't explain why — it just says she stayed. Why do you think the storytellers left it unexplained? What do you think her choice to stay means?

The gods felt something complicated about punishing Loki — he had been their companion and helper for so long. Have you ever had to hold someone accountable for something even when it was painful? How do you think the gods felt walking away from that cave?
Sigurd and the Dragon Fafnir: Fafnir warned Sigurd to leave the gold behind — told him directly that the curse was real and everyone who took it would be destroyed. Sigurd took it anyway. Why do you think he didn't listen? Have you ever been warned about something and chosen not to listen? What happened?

The curse on the gold didn't work through dramatic magic — it worked through ordinary human choices, each one understandable, all of them terrible together. What does that suggest about how the Norse thought curses and bad luck really operated in the world?

Sigurd killed the dragon, rode through fire, and woke Brynhild — but the story still ends in tragedy. Does a hero have to succeed in order to be a hero? What made Sigurd heroic, even if the ending was dark?
The Mead of Poetry: The Mead of Poetry was created from the spit of every god mixed together, which then became a being named Kvasir who knew everything. What do you think the Norse were saying about wisdom with that origin story — where does real wisdom come from, according to this myth?

Odin spent a whole summer doing the backbreaking work of nine men, then snuck into a mountain, then flew home in eagle form being chased by a giant — all for a drink. What does that effort say about how the Norse valued poetry and great storytelling?

At the very end, some mead spilled behind Odin during the chase — and the story says that became 'the portion for bad poets, the verse that almost works but doesn't quite.' What do you think that detail is really saying about the difference between good and bad writing?
The Valkyries: Choosers of the Slain: The Valkyries were looking for one specific thing: warriors who didn't flinch, even in hopeless situations. They weren't measuring strength or skill or whether you won. Why do you think unflinching courage — not victory — was the standard? Do you agree that's the right thing to measure?

Some Valkyries fell in love with mortal warriors and gave up their powers to be with them. What does it mean to give up something divine — something that makes you extraordinary — for someone you love? Do you think that kind of sacrifice is admirable or foolish?

The Norse warrior going into battle knew the Valkyries were watching — invisibly, above the smoke. How do you think that belief changed the way warriors fought? Does being watched — by someone who sees everything — change how you act? Should it?
The Viking Afterlife: Valhalla and Helhe: The Valkyries chose warriors for Valhalla based on courage, not on whether they won. A brave warrior on the losing side might be chosen; a coward on the winning side would not. What does that system say about what the Norse believed really mattered in battle — and in life?

Most people — farmers, craftsmen, mothers, merchants — went to Helheim, which was dim and quiet but not terrible. Only warriors who died bravely went to Valhalla. Does that seem fair to you? What might be missing from a system that only glorifies one kind of life?

The einherjar in Valhalla fought every day and feasted every night, preparing for Ragnarok — a battle most of them would lose. Would you want to spend your afterlife training for a battle you know you will probably lose? What does it say about the Norse that their ideal afterlife was purposeful rather than restful?
Ragnarök: The End and the Beginning: The Norse knew exactly how the world would end — who would fight whom, who would fall, who would survive. Odin knew his own death was coming and couldn't stop it. And yet he kept building Valhalla, kept gathering warriors, kept preparing. What does it mean to prepare for something you know you can't win?

After Ragnarok destroys everything, the world rises again — green, new, with Baldur returned and grain growing in fields nobody planted. Why do you think the Norse added this ending? What would the story feel like without it?

The Norse kept going — farming, sailing, fighting, building — even though they believed the world would end in fire and ice. Does knowing that something will eventually end make it more meaningful or less? How does Ragnarok change the way you think about the Norse people who believed it?
